coredata.dc:title: Phenylboronic acid functionalized high-crystallinity fluorescent covalent organic framework act as a sensing platform with dual performance for rifamycin antibiotics and water and adsorbent for rifamycin antibiotics

coredata.dc:description: Phenylboronic acid functionalized high-crystallinity fluorescent covalent organic framework (BA@COF), which can be applied for rifamycin antibiotics and water sensing, and for rifamycin antibiotics adsorption, was synthesized for the first time. To effectively form BA@COF by post-synthetic modification, a fluorescent COF (COF–OMe) was prepared using deep-eutectic solvent as solvent without using catalyst. The BA@COF showed good fluorescence property and adsorption capacity, which owned superior effects in detecting and adsorbing rifampicin, rifapentine, and rifabutin. The results of fluorescence quenching mechanisms showed that internal filtration effect, dynamic quenching, and photoinduced electron transfer were the major mechanisms. Moreover, mainly due to the pore selectivity of BA@COF, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interaction, BA@COF showed good selectivity for three antibiotics. Finally, a dual-function platform based on BA@COF was successfully developed for sensing and adsorbing three antibiotics from various samples. Besides, BA@COF also showed good effect to detect water amount in ethanol (0.1−50%, v/v).

head.ce:title: Phenylboronic acid functionalized high-crystallinity fluorescent covalent organic framework act as a sensing platform with dual performance for rifamycin antibiotics and water and adsorbent for rifamycin antibiotics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phenylboronic acid functionalized high-crystallinity fluorescent covalent organic framework (BA@COF), which can be applied for rifamycin antibiotics and water sensing, and for rifamycin antibiotics adsorption, was synthesized for the first time. To effectively form BA@COF by post-synthetic modification, a fluorescent COF (COF–OMe) was prepared using deep-eutectic solvent as solvent without using catalyst. The BA@COF showed good fluorescence property and adsorption capacity, which owned superior effects in detecting and adsorbing rifampicin, rifapentine, and rifabutin. The results of fluorescence quenching mechanisms showed that internal filtration effect, dynamic quenching, and photoinduced electron transfer were the major mechanisms. Moreover, mainly due to the pore selectivity of BA@COF, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interaction, BA@COF showed good selectivity for three antibiotics. Finally, a dual-function platform based on BA@COF was successfully developed for sensing and adsorbing three antibiotics from various samples. Besides, BA@COF also showed good effect to detect water amount in ethanol (0.1−50%, v/v).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, various safety problems such as food safety, environmental safety, and human safety caused by the antibiotics overuse or abuse have aroused global attention [1]. Rifamycin antibiotics, including rifampicin (RIF), rifapentine (RFT), and rifabutin (RBT), are a class of effective broad-spectrum antibiotics [2]. Research studies show that they can be used for the treatment of several different types of bacterial infections such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Staphylococcus aureus infection [3,4]. Especially, they are widely known because of their outstanding therapeutic advantages against tuberculosis, a disease with high morbidity and mortality [5]. However, the use of these drugs, especially in excess or over a long period of time, can bring a host of problems. In details, excessive consumption of RIF can lead to drug resistance and many adverse effects, such as nausea, vomiting, confusion, and liver toxicity [6]. RFT has hepatorenal toxicity, especially damage liver function, excessive or long-term use may also lead to anemia, hair loss, and other symptoms [7]. Besides, RBT has serious hematologic and ocular side-effects after prolonged dosing [8]. In addition, it is worth noting that in aquaculture, these rifamycin antibiotics especially RIF can be used to control bacterial diseases in aquatic organisms (e.g. fish and shrimp), while excessive use or abuse will lead to antibiotic-resistant genes in the pathogenic species in fish and boosting the antibiotic resistance [9]. More seriously, these excessive use or abuse will result in antibiotics accumulation in aquatic products and water environment, and then enter into human body through the food chain, causing harm to humans. However, these rifamycin antibiotics are not approved for use in aquatic organisms but they are widely used in actual aquaculture recently, resulting in huge risks. In this case, considering the harmfulness and the importance of rifamycin antibiotics, there is an urgent need for rapidly detect and adsorb rifamycin antibiotics in the aquatic organisms as well as in biological fluids effectively. Furthermore, monitoring of rifamycin antibiotics in the drugs is also necessary to ensure the quality of the drugs.

To date, numerous analytical techniques such as high performance liquid chromatography [10], liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry [11,12], and fluorescence method [13], for the direct detection of rifamycin antibiotics have already been developed. Among the mentioned methods, the fluorescence method is most preferable ascribing to its cost effectiveness, ease of fabrication, rapid response time, high selectivity, and sensitivity [14–17]. In particular, the development of fluorescent materials with adsorption function has pushed the development of fluorescence detection method to a higher level [18]. This kind of material can achieve the purpose of rapid detection of the target analytes through the change of fluorescence signal as well as adsorbing the target component. In recent years, a variety of fluorescent adsorption materials including fluorescent hydrogels [19], fluorescent organosilica-coated MOF nanoparticles [20], fluorescent covalent organic framework (fluorescent COFs) [21], and so on have been developed for various analytes. Among them, fluorescent COFs have become a potential material for the detection and adsorption due to the high stability, adjustable pore structure, large specific surface area, and modifiable functional group property [22]. Fluorescent COFs with various functional group characteristics can be developed according to the structural characteristics of target analytes, leading to the selective detection and adsorption of target analytes. To date, COFs which show both fluorescence and adsorption abilities have been prepared for detecting and adsorbing pesticides [23], cationic dyes [24], and so on. However, no fluorescent COF, which can be applied as sensing and adsorption dual-platform for rifamycin antibiotics, has been synthesized. Therefore, it is of great significance to design and fabricate a fluorescent COF for the detection and adsorption of rifamycin antibiotics from various samples.

Besides, as we all know, the existing fluorescent COFs are synthesized mostly in organic solvents and require catalysts [25]. The synthesis method is not environmentally friendly and the synthesis steps are a bit cumbersome. More critically, fluorescent COFs which synthesized these methods do not disperse well in solution, further preventing the follow-up detection and adsorption [26]. In our previous studies, we have already developed a catalyst-free green synthesis method by using deep-eutectic solvent (DES) as a solvent, which may have the potential to synthesize fluorescent COFs [27]. In addition, we have also adopted the post-synthetic modification method to produce fluorescent COFs with good dispersion property and good fluorescence characteristic [24]. Hence, the design and preparation of a fluorescent COF which has glorious fluorescence and good dispersity by using DES-based synthesized method followed by post-synthetic modification strategy for rifamycin antibiotics detection and adsorption are intriguing.

Inspired by above mentioned things, a phenylboronic acid functionalized fluorescent COF (named as BA@COF) with high-crystallinity by using choline chloride (ChCl)-hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP)-based DES as solvent followed by post-synthetic modification with (4-ethynylphenyl) boronic acid was developed for the first time. BA@COF exhibited prominent fluorescence responses and adsorption effects to RIF, RFT, and RBT, and thus, it can be simultaneously used for the detection and adsorption of rifamycin antibiotics. With the introduction of phenylboronic acid, BA@COF showed better dispersing performance in various solvents, higher fluorescence sensitivity and selectivity adsorption performances for three rifamycin antibiotics than COF–OMe. The studies on fluorescence quenching and selective adsorption mechanisms showed that rifamycin antibiotics mainly quenched the fluorescence of BA@COF through internal filtration effect (IFE), dynamic quenching, and photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Moreover, pore selectivity of BA@COF, hydrogen bond interaction, and π-π interaction mainly affected the selective adsorption abilities of BA@COF for three rifamycin antibiotics. Finally, BA@COF was successfully employed for the determination and adsorption of three rifamycin antibiotics from various actual samples including aquatic organisms, drugs, and blood. Besides, BA@COF also exhibited the ability to detect water in ethanol, and a wide detection range can be obtained (shown in Fig. 1 ).

1,3,5-tri(4-aminophenyl)benzene (purity>98%) and 2,5-dimethoxy-benzene-1, 4-diformaldehyde (>98%) were purchased from Jilin Chinese Academy of Sciences-Yanshen Technology Co., Ltd. RIF (>97%), RFT (>97%), rifabutin (RBT, >97%), (4-ethynylphenyl) boronic acid (>98%), boron trifluoride etherate (BF3·OEt2, >98%), and tetrachloro-1,4-benzoquinone (>98%) were gained from Shanghai Macklin Co., Ltd. Capreomycin (>99%) was purchased from Solarbio. Streptomycin sulfate (>98%), ofloxacin (>98%), norfloxacin (>98%), pefloxacin (>98%), moxifloxacin (>98%), and gatifloxacin (>98%) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Organic solvents and reagents related to various metal ions were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Choline chloride (ChCl) and HFIP were procured from Adamas Co., Ltd. All aqueous solutions were made with deionized water.

The synthesis of COF–OMe was carried out according to our previous reported method with some modification [27]. The details can be seen in Supporting Information. The synthesis of BA@COF was conducted according to the reported literature with some modifications [28]. In details, COF–OMe (20 mg), (4-ethynylphenyl) boronic acid (36.5 mg, 0.25 mmol), and tetrachloro-1,4-benzoquinone (40 mg, 0.15 mmol) were evenly dispersed in a mixture of BF3·OEt2 (20 μL, 0.15 mmol) and toluene (2 mL). After ultrasonic for 15 min, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL reactor and reacted at 110 °C for 72 h. Next, the products were separated through centrifugation and then were washed successively with tetrahydrofuran (THF, 10 mL × 3), ethanol (10 mL × 3), and deionized water (20 mL × 3). Finally, BA@COF was obtained after vacuum drying for 12 h at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of three rifamycin antibiotics
Anti-interference capability of BA@COF for rifamycin antibiotics detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to effectively use BA@COF for further detection of three rifamycin antibiotics, the anti-interference capability of BA@COF for drugs and metal ions, were assessed. In details, 300 μL of different metal ions (1 mM) or drugs (200 μg/mL, structures were displayed in Fig. S1) were added into 2.7 mL of BA@COF-ethanol dispersed solution (100 μg/mL). The fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer LS-55) was used to determine the fluorescence intensity of the solution at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of BA@COF to three rifamycin antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: 300 μL of different concentrations of RIF, RFT, or RBT were separately added into 2.7 mL of BA@COF-ethanol dispersed solution (100 μg/mL). After blowing the solution 10 times, the fluorescence intensity was measured under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Three experiments were conducted in parallel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption performances of BA@COF to three RIF antibiotics

ce:section.ce:para: To measure the adsorption abilities of BA@COF to RIF, RFT, and RBT, BA@COF (1 mg) was separately added into 1 mL of RIF, RFT, and RBT water solution at different concentrations (0–500 μg/mL). After shaking the solution for 60 min, the supernatant filtered by using a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane was used for high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis. The adsorption capacities of BA@ COF to three RIF antibiotics were calculated using the formula shown in Supporting Information.

1 mg of BA@COF was put into 1 mL of the RIF antibiotic solution (100 μg/mL). Then, the solution was shaken in a warping shaker for a period of times (0–180 min). After the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane, the supernatant was analyzed using HPLC. The adsorption capacities of BA@ COF to three RIF antibiotics were also calculated using the formula shown in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BA@COF in actual samples
Application of BA@COF in aquatic organisms
ce:section.ce:para: Basa fish and shrimp (purchased from supermarket in Luzhou, Sichuan province) were selected as aquatic organisms. First, part of peeled Basa fish or shrimp was put into a mortar and grounded until homogenized. Next, 200 mL of ethanol (for fluorescence detection) or pure water (for adsorption) was severally added into 20 g of fish homogenates. Ultrasound was carried out for 5 min until the homogenate was mixed with the solution. After filtration by filter paper, RIF, RFT, or RBT was added into the filtrate to form sample solution of different concentrations (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL), respectively. For fluorescence detection, 300 μL of sample solution was added into 2.7 mL of BA@COF–ethanol-dispersed solution (100 μg/mL). After ten blows with the pipette, the fluorescence intensity was determined. All experiments were repeated three times. For adsorption, the steps were same with the steps in the part of adsorption isotherms.

Three drugs, including RIF capsule, RIF eye drop tablet, and RFT capsule, were selected as the actual drugs to assess the practical application potential of BA@COF. In details, ten capsules (Total weight of 1.5 g) with RIF or RFT standard dosage of 0.15 g per capsule were open and powder inside was gained. Then, weighing 15 mg of powder was dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol to prepare a solution of RIF or RFT (1 mg/mL). One piece of RIF eye drop tablet (5 mg, 10 mL buffer) was ground to a powder (The standard dosage of 5 mg per tablet), and then the powder was dissolved in 5 mL ethanol to prepare a solution with the concentration of RIF of 1 mg/mL. Next, each solution was ultrasonic mixed and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. After that, diluting each solution and the fluorescence detection was carried out according to the steps described in Section 2.3.2. The HPLC method was used for comparison. Specific steps can be seen in Supporting Information.

Further, for adsorption, the solution configuration procedures were same with above mentioned in the steps of fluorescence detection of drugs except that the dissolving solvent was changed to water. The adsorption steps were same with the steps mentioned in the part of adsorption isotherms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BA@COF in blood sample
ce:section.ce:para: Adult male venous plasma was used as blood sample. Before doing fluorescence detection, the standard curves for fluorescence detection in blood sample were established. Details were shown in Supporting Information. Since no RIF and RFT were detected in blood samples, the standard addition method would be employed to verify the suitability of the fluorescence method. In details, RIF or RFT was separately added into plasma to form sample solutions of different concentrations (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL). After pretreatment (Steps were same with those in the pretreatment steps in the establishment of standard curves), the fluorescence detection was done according to the steps described in Section 2.3.2. For adsorption, after the preparation of sample solution of different concentration (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL), another 100 μL of water was added into each sample so that the BA@COF (1 mg) can be completely immersed into the sample solution. After adsorption for 30 min, the BA@COF was separated through centrifugation, and 600 μL of acetonitrile was used to elute the RIF or RFT which adsorbed on BA@COF. After completely desorbed, each eluent was dried with N2 after centrifugation at 10000 r/min for 5 min and redissolved with 100 μL water for HPLC detection.
The conditions of HPLC analysis were described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of water content in ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: BA@COF was ground and dispersed in ethanol to form BA@COF-ethanol solution (100 μg/mL). After adding different content of water into the solution, the fluorescence measurement was done.

The design and synthesis processes of BA@COF were shown in Fig. 1. To be specific, BA@COF was synthesized by using ChCl–HFIP-based DES as a solvent by a Schiff base reaction heated at 120 °C for 72 h between 2,5-dimethoxy-benzene-1, 4-diformaldehyde and 1,3,5-tri(4-aminophenyl)benzene (COF–OMe), followed by a cycloaddition between (4-ethynylphenyl) boric acid and COF–OMe at 110 °C. To verify the structure of BA@COF, comprehensive approaches such as powder X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), N2 adsorption–desorption analysis, and water contact angle were performed.

First, to evaluate the successful preparation of BA@COF, the powder X-ray diffraction patterns, FT-IR spectra, and XPS spectra of COF–OMe and BA@COF were done and compared. As shown in Fig. 2 A, COF–OMe showed peaks at about 2.81°, 4.78°, 5.61°, 7.38°, 9.74°, and 25.51°, which were associated with (100), (110), (200), (210), (220), and (001), respectively. The maximum peak in XRD spectrum of COF–OMe was 2.81°, which was its (100) crystal plane, indicating that COF-OME had a layered stacking structure. This result was consistent with previously reports, suggesting that the desired COF–OMe with high-crystallinity was successfully prepared [28,29]. The potential structure of COF–OMe was further confirmed by theoretical simulation experiment. Structural model was constructed using Materials Studio (MS) to elucidate the conformation of COF–OMe. As shown in Fig. S2, the structure of COF–OMe was consistent with the AA-stacking mode rather than AB-stacking mode. Moreover, it is obviously seen that BA@COF showed similar diffraction peaks with COF–OMe, indicating that good crystallinity of BA@COF was also maintained. The results (Fig. 2B) of FT-IR spectra of COF–OMe and BA@COF showed that compared with the spectrum of COF–OMe, the spectrum of BA@COF showed three new peaks at 1358, 1195, and 761 cm−1, which were assigned to stretching vibrations of B–O, C–B, and bending vibration of O–H, respectively [28]. These phenomena revealed that the boronic acid moiety was introduced into BA@COF as expected. Further, the XPS spectra (Fig. 2C and Fig. S3) revealed that BA@COF had new signals at 191.73 eV and 193.79 eV, which were attributed to boron elements. Besides, Figs. S4A–B showed that compared N 1s spectra of COF–OMe and BA@COF, the number of peaks in N 1s of BA@COF increased from two to three, the location and attribution of the peaks also changed. In details, the additional peak at 399.5 eV was assigned to quinoline formed after cycloaddition, demonstrating the successful conversion of the imine to quinoline in BA@COF. Moreover, two peaks of 399.6 eV and 398.8 eV which were attributed to N–H and imine bonds appeared in the spectrum of COF–OMe, moved to 400.9 eV and 398.6 eV in the spectrum of BA@COF, respectively. Besides, based on the integration of the peak areas, the degree of functionalized (4-ethynylphenyl) boronic acid of on BA@COF was assessed to be 63.97%.

The morphologies of BA@COF were characterized by scanning electron microscopy and high-resolution TEM. It can be easily seen that BA@COF had spherical shape with protrusions (Fig. 2D), which was much rougher than that of COF–OMe (Fig. S5A). HR-TEM images (Fig. 2E–F) further showed the fine structure of BA@COF. It can be known that BA@COF was chrysanthemum-shaped, and it had a very distinct lattice structure. BA@COF had a lattice spacing of about 2.3 nm, corresponding to (100) crystal plane (Fig. 2F) [30]. Moreover, this lattice spacing was comparable to that of COF–OMe (Fig. S5B), indicating that the introduction of (4-ethynyl-acid phenyl) boronic acid did not alter the original crystal structure of COF–OMe. These results further indicated that BA@COF also had high-crystallinity. To further analyze the properties of BA@COF, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were carried out. Apparently, Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area decreased from 159 m2 g−1 for COF–OMe to 143 m2 g−1 for BA@COF (Figure S5C and Fig. 2G). The reason for this phenomenon may be that boronic acid groups were grafted into the pores of COF–OMe. Furthermore, the pore sizes of COF–OMe and BA@COF were detected as 37.7826 nm and 26.65 nm, respectively. These results demonstrated that post-functionalization of imine-linked COF was successfully achieved by cycloaddition.

TGA was also performed to investigate the thermal stability of the as-prepared materials. As described in Fig. 2H, COF–OMe exhibited good thermal stability at about 200 °C with a weight loss of 9.66%. However, BA@COF showed good thermal stability at about 300 °C with a weight loss of 5.33%, which was ascribed to the desorption of the solvent and moisture inside the COF. When the temperature was raised to 800 °C, two significant weight losses (about 35.53% and 12.65%) were observed in the TGA curve of COF–OMe, which was due to the broken of the COF backbone. BA@COF had a smaller weight loss (about 26.14%) than COF–OMe. These results showed that the thermal stability of BA@COF was better than that of COF–OMe. In addition, in order to access the hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of materials, contact angle experiments were done. As displayed in Figure S5D and Fig. 2I, COF–OMe was hydrophilic, while BA@COF was hydrophobic. The reason for this result may be the introduction of (4-ethynylphenyl) boric acid in the structure of COF–OMe changed the property of COF–OMe as (4-ethynylphenyl) boric acid was hydrophobic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of three RIF antibiotics using BA@COF
Fluorescence property and stability of BA@COF in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: As BA@COF had different fluorescence properties in different solvents, the fluorescence properties and stability of BA@COF in various solvents were explored at first. Fig. 3 A and Fig. S6 displayed that under the same concentration, BA@COF showed strongest fluorescence in ethanol, demonstrating that ethanol can be used as the dispersing solvent for the detection of three RIF antibiotics in further experiments. Next, the stability of BA@COF in ethanol was explored through detecting its fluorescence change within 6 h. As shown in Fig. S7A, BA@COF was stable in ethanol, little decrease of fluorescence intensity can be seen within 6 h. Further, to investigate whether the structure of BA@COF would be affected in different solvents, FT-IR spectra were carried out. It is clearly seen that after soaking in various solvents including water, ethanol, acetonitrile, 0.1 M HCl, and 0.1 M NaOH for 24 h, no significant changes can be seen from their FT-IR spectra (Fig. S7B), revealing BA@COF showed good stability in various solvents.

RIF antibiotics are mainly used in tuberculosis medication. However, most of the common clinical anti-tuberculosis antibiotics are RIF antibiotics, streptomycin, quinolone antibiotics, and aminoglycoside antibiotics [31,32]. In addition, metal ions may also interfere with the testing of the target RIF antibiotics in aquatic organisms and blood samples. Therefore, it is necessary to evaluate the antijamming capabilities of BA@COF for these components. First, to verify the antijamming capabilities of BA@COF for other antibiotics, different antibiotics were selected and dissolved in ethanol. As shown in Fig. 3B, only RIF, RFT, and RBT can effectively quench the fluorescence of BA@COF, while other antibiotics almost showed no influence on the fluorescence intensities of BA@COF. These results suggested that BA@COF had good antijamming capabilities for other antibiotics and showed high selectivity for RIF, RFT, and RBT. Besides, the antijamming capabilities of BA@COF for metal ions were also conducted. Obviously, Fig. 3C showed that all metal ions showed little influences on the fluorescence intensities of BA@COF, revealing the good antijamming capabilities of BA@COF for metal ions. Thus, BA@COF can be further used for the detection of RIF, RFT, and RBT in different samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivities of BA@COF for three RIF antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that BA@COF showed good selectivity for RIF, RFT, and RBT, it can be applied as a fluorescent platform for their detection. The sensitivities of BA@COF for RIF, RFT, and RBT were investigated by analyzing the fluorescence responses of BA@COF to RIF, RFT, and RBT with different concentrations. In order to conduct the detection at the best conditions, the fluorescence detection times of BA@COF for RIF, RFT, and RBT were investigated at first. The results indicated that within 30 s, the fluorescence quenching of RIF, RFT, and RBT for BA@COF became stable, demonstrating that fluorescence responses of BA@COF to them were rapid (Fig. S8). Thus, 30 s was selected as the incubation time in further detection. Figs. 3D–F revealed that the fluorescence intensities of BA@COF decreased gradually with the increase of the concentration from 0 to 1000 μg/mL for RIF, RFT, and RBT. The linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity of BA@COF and the concentration of RIF, RFT, and RBT were separately obtained in the concentration ranges of 0.1–200 μg/mL, 0.1–200 μg/mL, and 0.1–100 μg/mL, with R2 = 0.9905, 0.9930, and 0.9965, respectively (Figs. 3G–I). The resulting linear equations were calculated as y = 0.0026x + 0.0867, y = 0.0025x + 0.0515, y = 0.0023x + 0.0804, where y = (F0 − F)/F0, x was the concentration of the target analyte. Their limits of detection (LODs) gained by the generalized three times the standard deviations of the measurements were all 0.03 μg/mL. Further, to verify the merits of this BA@COF for the detection of three RIF antibiotics, the current results were compared with previously literature (Shown in Table S1 in Supporting Information). Obviously, BA@COF-based fluorescent sensor can be successfully used to detect three RIF antibiotics with satisfactory LODs, further revealing that BA@COF could be employed to detect RIF antibiotics in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison the fluorescence performances of COF–OMe and BA@COF for three RIF antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate whether the fluorescence sensitivity of COF–OMe to three RIF antibiotics could be significantly improved by the cycloaddition, the fluorescence detection performances of two materials to three RIF antibiotics were compared. As shown in Figs. S9A–S9C, under the same detection condition, BA@COF showed more significantly fluorescence quenching effects to three RIF antibiotics than those of COF–OMe, demonstrating that the fluorescence sensitivities of BA@COF to RIF, RFT, and RBT were much higher than those of COF–OMe. These results further indicated that BA@COF has high application potential in fluorescence detection of three RIF antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of three RIF antibiotics by BA@COF

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the adsorption abilities of BA@COF to three RIF antibiotics, adsorption isotherms and kinetics experiments were conducted. As displayed in Fig. 3J, with the concentrations of RIF, RFT, and RBT increased from 0 to 200 μg/mL, the adsorption capacities of BA@COF for them gradually increased. Moreover, the maximum adsorption capacities for RIF, RFT, and RBT were determined as 146.69, 198.64, and 44.47 mg/g, respectively. In comparison with the reported adsorbents for the adsorption of these RIF antibiotics [33,34], the adsorption performances of BA@COF were satisfactory.

To deeply understand the adsorption behaviors of BA@COF, Langmuir and Freundlich models, which described in Supporting Information, were applied. Table S2 and Fig. S10 separately listed the values of the corresponding parameters and the specific fitting curves. It is clearly seen that Langmuir isotherm was more suitable for describing the adsorption behaviors of three RIF antibiotics than the Freundlich isotherm model compared to the r value, demonstrating that three RIF antibiotics can adsorb more easily and uniformly on porous scaffolds.

The adsorption kinetic curves for RIF, RFT, and RBT at different adsorption times were exhibited in Fig. 3K. The results showed that the adsorption of RIF, RFT, and RBT can reach equilibrium at about 30 min. The fast adsorption effects may be related to the good adsorption performances of BA@COF for three RIF antibiotics. Thus, 30 min was selected as the adsorption time in further experiments. Further, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models (formulas shown in Supporting Information) were used to explore the adsorption behavior of RIF, RFT, and RBT on BA@COF. As depicted in Table S3 and Fig. S11, compared with the r value, the pseudo-second-order model was more suitable to describe the adsorption of three RIF antibiotics by using BA@COF, indicating that chemical interaction may be involved in the adsorption process.

As reported, the increase of temperature increases the possibility of collision of target molecule with adsorbent in a certain range, and the adsorption process is often accompanied by energy conversion [35,36]. Thus, the thermodynamic model can be applied to further explore the adsorption processes of BA@COF on three RIF antibiotics. Different thermodynamic parameters, including enthalpy of adsorption (ΔH0), entropy of adsorption (ΔS0), and free energy of adsorption (ΔG0), were gained by conducting the adsorption experiments at different temperatures (30, 35, and 40 °C). According to equations described in Supporting Information, it can be calculated that ΔH > 0, ΔS > 0, and ΔG < 0, confirming that the adsorption of three RIF antibiotics on BA@COF was endothermic, randomly increasing, and spontaneous processes (Fig. 3L and Table S4) [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison the adsorption performances of COF–OMe and BA@COF for three RIF antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly, with the study of the fluorescence performances, the adsorption performances of COF–OMe and BA@COF for three RIF antibiotics were also compared. As displayed in Fig. S12, under the same adsorption conditions, the adsorption efficiencies of BA@COF for three RIF antibiotics were significantly higher than those of COF–OMe, indicating that the post-functionalized BA@COF had better adsorption performances for three RIF antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BA@COF in actual samples
Application of BA@COF in aquatic organisms
ce:section.ce:para: As reported, RIF antibiotics such as RIF are not approved for animal use, and it is illegal to add these drugs to animal feed and drinking water [38]. However, because these drugs are inexpensive and have good antimicrobial effects, they often have been illegally used in aquatic organisms. Thus, the presence of these drugs in aquatic organisms needs to be monitored. Herein, Basa fish and shrimp, two of most common consumed aquatic products, were used to evaluate the practical application potential of BA@COF in the detection and adsorption of three RIF antibiotics in actual aquatic organisms. The analytical performances of BA@COF for the fluorescence detection of RIF, RFT, and RBT at three concentrations (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL) in ethanol containing fish and shrimp homogenates were measured. As summarized in Table 1 , the detection recoveries for three RIF antibiotics ranged from 75.20% to 123.46%. The low relative standard deviations (RSDs) (<4.46%) suggested that BA@COF can be successfully used for the detection of RIF, RFT, and RBT in actual aquatic organisms. Besides, the adsorption properties of BA@COF for RIF, RFT, and RBT were also measured at three concentrations (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL) in water containing fish and shrimp homogenates. The results in Table S5 revealed that the adsorption recoveries of three RIF antibiotics were 70.52%–100.04%. Moreover, the low RSDs (<6.85%) also showed that BA@COF could be successfully applied for the adsorption of RIF, RFT, and RBT. In general, the methods for fluorescence detection and adsorption of three RIF antibiotics in fish and shrimp were established, which is of great practical significance for strictly monitoring and removing RIF antibiotics.

In clinical practice, anti-tuberculosis antibiotics are commonly used, RIF capsule, RFT capsule, and RIF eye drop (Treatment of eye disease). Thus, these three kinds of drugs were selected as the actual samples to access the practical application potential of BA@COF in drugs. Based on BA@COF, the fluorescence detection methods of RIF in capsule and eye drop tablet, and RFT in capsule were constructed through the above-mentioned method in Section 2.5.2. The results were listed in Table 2 . It is obviously seen that the contents of RIF and RFT in each capsule were separately determined as 153.90 mg and 152.50 mg using the fluorescence method, which were separately 102.60% and 101.67% of that of the labeled amount of per capsule (150 mg). Satisfactory RSDs were also gained in range of 1.07%–9.34%. Moreover, the content of RIF in eye drop tablet was determined as 5.03 mg, which was 100.60% (RSD was 8.30%) of the labeled amount (5 mg). In order to verify the practicality of the fluorescence detection method, the HPLC method was also used to detect the contents of RIF and RFT in related samples. As displayed in Table 2, the contents of RIF in capsule and eye drop table, and RFT in capsule were separately detected as 150.04, 4.93, and 150.20 mg, which were severally 100.03%, 98.60%, and 100.13% of the labeled amounts. The RSDs of the HPLC methods ranged from 0.67% to 5.71%. In comparison with the results of two methods, it can be easily known that the results of the HPLC method were consistent with those of the fluorescence detection method, verifying the effectiveness of the developed fluorescence detection method. Besides, as far as we know, according to the Chinese Pharmacopoeia, the content of RIF in the capsule should be 90.0%–110.0% of the labeled amount. The obtained results also meet the requirements of Chinese Pharmacopoeia, further identifying the effectiveness of the established fluorescence detection method.

Furthermore, to evaluate the adsorption abilities of BA@COF in various drugs, the adsorption performances of BA@COF in different concentration (10, 50, and 100 μg/mL) of target drugs in water were measured. The results in Table S6 demonstrated that the recoveries for three drugs were between 64.71% and 100.00% with low RSDs (<5.92%), indicating that BA@COF dispersed in water can be successfully used in the adsorption of in various drugs.

RIF capsule and RFT capsule are commonly used as first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs in clinic, while long-term use or combination with other anti-tuberculosis drugs in clinical cases often appear hepatotoxicity, gastrointestinal discomfort, allergic reactions, drug resistance, and so on. [39]. Moreover, because of the cost and the concern about toxicity, RIF was administered orally at a daily dose of 600 mg (8–12 mg/kg body weight). At this dose, clinical trials in the 1970s found cure rates of ≥95% and recurrence rates of <5% [40]. Based on the effective oral dose, venous blood from adult males was used as the blood sample for the detection of RIF and RFT in vitro by the spiking method. The analytical performances of BA@COF in detecting two drugs at three concentrations (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL) in adult male venous blood were done. The results revealed that the recoveries of RIF and RFT were in the range of 94.99%–123.48% (Table 3 ). The low RSDs (<5.06%) demonstrated that BA@COF could be successfully used for the detection of RIF and RFT in human blood, further revealing that the fluorescence detection method which based BA@COF had the potential to detect the change of concentrations for RIF capsule and RFT capsule in blood.

In addition, in the experiment which BA@COF was used as an adsorbent for the adsorption of RIF and RFT in blood, it can be found that the adsorption efficiencies of BA@COF for two drugs were between 46.86% and 56.05%, which was not as good as expected (Table S7). The reason for the low adsorption recoveries may be due to the presence of some plasma proteins, which caused the blockage of some pores of BA@COF, thus affecting the adsorption of BA@COF. However, it is clear that the adsorption effect of BA@COF was still up to 50%, indicating that BA@COF can be still used for the adsorption of RIF and RFT in human blood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching and selective mechanisms of BA@COF to three RIF antibiotics

ce:section.ce:para: As reported, quenching mechanisms contain static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence response energy transfer, PET, and IFE [41]. Figs. 4 A–D showed that the fluorescence lifetime of BA@COF (Dispersed in ethanol) was 7.62 ns, and the fluorescence lifetimes of BA@COF with RIF, RFT, and RBT were determined as 2.45 ns, 2.28 ns, and 2.46 ns, respectively. Because the statistical changes in lifetimes were pronounced, dynamic quenching rather than static quenching can be determined [42]. To explore the existence of IFE, the fluorescence spectra of BA@COF which dispersed in ethanol and ultraviolet visible absorption spectra of three RIF antibiotics were explored. It was found from Fig. 4E that the fluorescence spectra of BA@COF overlapped with the ultraviolet visible absorption spectra of three RIF antibiotics, indicating that IFE happened [41]. Further, Stern-Volmer equation was also employed (Formulas shown in Supporting Information). Figs. 4F–H showed that Ksv gradually increased with the increase of the temperature. Moreover, the Kq values at 20, 30, and 40 °C were lower than the range of 1–2 × 1010 L/mol/s, revealing that the quenching happened through a dynamic quenching process. In general, IFE and dynamic quenching may be two fluorescence quenching mechanisms.

In addition, the high quenching efficiency of RIF, RFT, and RBT to the fluorescence of BA@COF may also be attributed to the π-π interaction between the three RIF antibiotics and the repeating units of BA@COF and their electronic structure matching. In principle, the quenching process can be regarded as PET [43]. Photoinduced electrons on highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of BA@COF (donor) (Black spheres shown in Fig. S13A) can be transferred to their adjacent electron acceptors (e.g. RIF and RFT) and then electrons on acceptor’ HOMO can rebind to those of electrons on donor’ HOMO through a non-radiating process. Besides, the photoinduced electrons on lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of BA@COF (donor) (White spheres shown in Fig. S13B) can also be transferred to their adjacent electron acceptors (e.g. RBT) and then the electrons on the LUMO of the acceptor can rebind to the electrons on HOMO of the donor through a non-radiative process. Therefore, it can be expected that the electron transfer may occur accompanied with the excitation if the electron-deficient analyte’ LUMO and HOMO exist between the sensor’ HOMO and LUMO [43]. When the sensor is excited, the charge can be transferred from the HOMO or LUMO of the sensor to the HOMO of the RIF, RFT, or the LUMO of the RBT, leading to the quenching of the initial fluorescence. To verify these assumptions, Dmol3 module density functional theory (DFT) via MS was used to estimate the energy of HOMO (LUMO) of BA@COF as well as those of RIF, RFT, and RBT. The results showed that in all cases, the HOMO of RIF and RFT, the LUMO of RBT located in HOMO or LUMO of BA@COF (Shown in Fig. S14). Therefore, excited state electron transfer existed between BA@COF with RIF, RFT, and RBT. Moreover, the energy differences in LUMO and HOMO of BA@COF, RIF, RFT, and RBT indicated that these three RIF antibiotics can have better fluorescence quenching abilities than other anti-tuberculosis antibiotics. Further, to identify the real charge transfer mechanism between BA@COF with RIF, RFT, or RBT, a battery of computational calculations was performed by DFT. Geometric optimization of the sensors in the presence of RIF, RFT, or RBT showed that the most favorable position (in terms of energy) for the interaction between RIF, RFT, RBT with BA@COF was near the pyridine N (Fig. S15). If RIF, RFT, or RBT formed charge transfer complexes with BA@COF, the energy of LUMO as well as HOMO of the newly formed complexes should be smaller in comparison with those of BA@COF. To further understand the fluorescence behaviors between BA@COF and three RIF antibiotics, MS program was used to perform DFT calculations using the DMol3 module. Frontier molecular orbitals were derived from the optimized geometry. The representative energy maps (Figs. 5 A–C) showed that the initial HOMO and LUMO energy difference of BA@COF reduced in the presence of RIF, RFT, and RBT. During this process, the energy of the HOMO of BA@COF separately decreased by no less than 1.726 eV and 1.642 eV to the newly formed HOMO of the [BA@COF + RIF] and [BA@COF + RFT] adducts. The LOMO energy did not change significantly (only 0.168 eV and 0.132 eV) (Fig. 5A–B). The energy of the LUMO of the BA@COF decreased by no less than 1.426 eV to the newly formed LUMO of the [BA@COF + RBT] adduct, while the HOMO energy did not change significantly (only 0.385 eV) (Fig. 5C). These results showed that the frontier band gap energies of BA@COF and three RIF antibiotics complexes were reduced. It can also be noticed that the BA@COF driving forces (ΔG) were 1.762 eV, 1.642 eV, and 1.426 eV for RIF, RFT, and RBT, respectively. According to the computational program of MS, the internal recombination energy (λi) was also calculated. The λi values of [BA@COF + RIF], [BA@COF + RFT], and [BA@COF + RFT] were separately calculated as 4.066 eV, 3.593 eV, and 5.458 eV (Detail descriptions of ΔG and λi were shown in Supporting Information). According to the generalized Born method (shown in Supporting Information), it can be seen that the electronic coupling term strongly depends on the strength of the D-A interaction, which means that the close contact of D and A molecules is necessary for the effective interaction of the two molecules. π-π interactions can be formed between RIF, RFT, and RBT with BA@COF. Only by doing so, the electron coupling term (V) and driving force (ΔG) became important for the electron transfer rate. Therefore, in BA@COF-RIF antibiotics systems, three RIF antibiotics must diffuse into the covalent organic backbone layer and formed effective π-π superimposed D-A aggregation, and only in this way, the electronic coupling term became important. Briefly, PET also played an important role in the fluorescence quenching of BA@COF.

Size and functional groups selectivity of COF are important for their selectivity to analytes. Thus, in order to explore the size selectivity of BA@COF, the sizes of capreomycin, ofloxacin, norfloxacin, pefloxacin, moxifloxacin, gatifloxacin, streptomycin, RIF, RFT, and RBT were compared. Fig. S16 showed that the molecular sizes of RIF, RFT, and RBT were separately determined as18.466 Å, 19.526 Å, and 19.146 Å, which were about two-third of the pore size of BA@COF (26.59 Å). These analytes with a size of approximately two-third of COF’ pore size is relatively easy to enter in the pore of COF and easily adsorb on COF through some forces. Therefore, RIF, RFT, and RBT can be easily adsorbed on BA@COF through several forces. However, the sizes of ofloxacin, norfloxacin, pefloxacin, moxifloxacin, gatifloxacin, and streptomycin were much smaller, they may directly pass through the pore of BA@COF, and thus they showed little effects. Besides, although the size of capreomycin was similar with RIF, RFT, and RBT, the poor effect with BA@COF was also obtained. The reason for this may be that the structural functional groups of capreomycin differ considerably from the three RIF antibiotics, making its action weak with BA@COF. In general, the pore size selectivity of BA@COF can be regarded as one of the main causes for the selectivity toward three RIF antibiotics by using BA@COF.

Moreover, it has been reported that the selectivity of COFs for target analytes is related to the different forces brought by COFs and functional groups on the structures of analytes (such as π-π forces, hydrogen bonding forces, etc.). These different forces also brought differences in adsorption capacity. The selectivity mechanisms of three RIF antibiotics were described in Figs. 5D–F. Obviously, due to the phenyl group as donor and the pyridine N as acceptor, BA@COF showed an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). The π-π bond in three RIF antibiotics showed high electron density energy supply capacity on the benzene ring, which enabled the phenol hydroxyl group to form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl group and pyridine N on BA@COF better, and thus destroying the ICT effect. The cores factors that disrupted ICT or formed hydrogen bonds were the existence of π-π bonds or the differences of the hydrogen bond sites (the location of the phenol hydroxyl group) in the structure of three RIF antibiotics. To identify the importance of π-π and hydrogen bonds in RIF antibiotics, capreomycin was selected to assess this effect because the structural difference between it with RIF lied in π-π and hydrogen-bond interactions brought by the naphthalene ring of the structure. Compared with the effects of BA@COF on them (Fig. 3B), BA@COF had almost no effect on capreomycin, while it had good effects on three RIF antibiotics. This may indicate that the naphthalene ring in the structure was the key structure affecting the effect of BA@COF.

In addition, the position of hydrogen bond which interacted with BA@COF on naphthalene ring of three RIF antibiotics also affected the interaction between them. This effect can be analyzed from the structures of RIF, RFT, and RBT and their effects with BA@COF. As shown in Fig. 3J, BA@COF showed better adsorption effects on RIF, RFT rather than RBT. The functional groups of RBT on the branch chain of naphthalene ring are different from those of RIF and RFT. Fig. S17 showed that the sites that could form hydrogen bonds with BA@COF on the naphthalene ring branch chain of RBT (marked in red in Fig. S17) were significantly less than those of RIF and RFT (Marked in red in Fig. S17), leading to its relatively poor effect. Furthermore, it can be known that electrostatic interaction may also affect the interaction between BA@COF and three RIF antibiotics. In order to investigate whether this effect was another major effect between BA@COF and three RIF antibiotics, their electrostatic potentials were also analyzed. As can be seen from Fig. S18, the electrostatic interaction also had a certain effect, while it was not the dominant role. These all results indicated that the pore selectivity of BA@COF, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interaction played dominant roles, while the electrostatic interactions played a minor role.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of water in ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: In the previous experiment, we found that the presence of water in ethanol would reduce the fluorescence intensity of BA@COF. Thus, to explore this effect, BA@COF was dispersed in ethanol and different content of water was added. Before sensing, the fluorescence detection time was investigated. Fig. S8 showed that 3.5 min can be used as the detection time. Moreover, Figs. S19A–S19B revealed that the fluorescence of BA@COF gradually quenched with the increase of water content in ethanol. A good linear relationship was gained between F0/F and water content ranged from 1% to 50% (v/v). In comparison with other reported literature [44,45], BA@COF showed a wide detection range (0.1−50%) of water in ethanol and satisfactory LOD (0.03%). To further understanding the sensing mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime and Stern-Volmer equation were done. As displayed in Fig. S20, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of water may also be IFE and dynamic quenching.

Reusability reflected the performance of BA@COF for sensing. Thus, the reusability of BA@COF was conducted. Fig. S21 showed that the fluorescence responses of BA@COF to analytes were not significantly affected after five recycles, revealing the good reusability of BA@COF.

Herein, phenylboronic acid functionalized fluorescent COF (BA@COF) with high crystalline was successfully synthesized and employed for the detection and adsorption of three RIF antibiotics from various samples, as well as for the detection of water content in ethanol. The BA@COF-based sensing platform showed various advantages such as good selectivity, comparable sensitivity, and high adsorption capacities to three RIF antibiotics. The systematic mechanism studies showed that IFE, dynamic quenching, and PET were the major fluorescence quenching mechanisms. The pore selectivity of BA@COF, hydrogen bonding and π-π interaction played dominant roles in the selective adsorption of three RIF antibiotics. Further, the applications of BA@COF for the detection and adsorption of RIF, RFT, and RBT in aquatic organism, drugs and plasma samples gained satisfactory results. In addition, this is the first report that BA@COF was used to the sensing of water in ethanol with a relatively wide content range (0.1%–50% in ethanol, v/v). In general, the developed method for the detection and adsorption of RIF antibiotics in actual samples by using BA@COF and the method for water sensing in ethanol show great promise.
